content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 19, 2016

Richard D. Pio Roda
San Leandro City Attorney
555 12th Street, Suite 1500
Oakland, CA 94607

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-061

Dear Mr. Pio Roda:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of San Leandro Rent
Review Board Chair Thomas Silva regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Chair Silva appear as a member of the general public before the City of San Leandro
Rent Review Board to represent himself in a rent review hearing regarding a potential rent increase
at Newport Apartments, an apartment complex in which he and his wife have a 60% ownership
interest, given that he has a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding that decision under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Chair Silva may appear at the hearing as a member of the general public on matters
related solely to his personal interests, but otherwise he must comply with the procedures for
disqualification set forth in Section 87105.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of San Leandro and the authorized representative of
Rent Review Board Chair Thomas Silva.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-061
Page No. 2

The City has adopted a Rent Review Ordinance, and under the Ordinance, a tenant that
resides in a covered rental unit within the City is entitled to request a rent review hearing upon
receiving notice of a qualifying rent increase from his or her landlord. A rent increase qualifies for
review under the Ordinance if it is: (1) greater than $75; (2) greater than 10% of the rent paid during
the prior month; or (3) within 12 months of a prior rent increase. Rent review hearings are held
before the City's Rent Review Board. Members of the Board are appointed by the City Council, and
Chair Silva is an appointed member of the Board.

If a tenant in a covered rental unit receives notice of a qualifying rent increase and requests a
rent review hearing, the Ordinance requires the landlord and tenant to appear at the rent review
hearing. Participation in the rent review hearing is mandatory for landlords and tenants that seek
review. If the tenant fails to appear, the rent increase goes into effect without further review of the
proposed increase. If the landlord fails to appear, the rent increase does not go into effect. If the
landlord fails to appear and wishes to continue to pursue the rent increase, the landlord must re-
notice the tenant of the rent increase in compliance with California law and the Ordinance.

At the rent review hearing, the landlord and the tenant are provided an opportunity to
explain their respective positions regarding the rent increase. The Board can consider factors,
including, but not limited to: the hardship to the tenant, the frequency and amount of prior rent
increases, the landlord's mortgage payments and other costs associated with owning and
maintaining the property, the landlord's interest in earning a reasonable rate of return, and any other
factors that may assist the Board in determining a fair resolution to the dispute.

Following the rent review hearing, the Board makes a non-binding recommendation to the
parties for the resolution of their dispute. If the parties agree to a resolution proposed by the Board,
they may formalize the agreement. The Board can continue a rent review matter for one final
continuance hearing, refer the matter to the City Council, or dismiss the matter. The Ordinance does
not limit rent increases or authorize any City body to restrict rent increases.

Chair Silva and his wife own a 60% stake in Newport Apartments, and an individual living
outside of California owns the remaining 40%. The Bylaws for Newport Apartments provide that the
majority owner controls its direction. Tenants at Newport Apartments may request a rent review hearing
for qualifying rent increases under the Ordinance, and a tenant at Newport Apartments recently
requested a rent review hearing for a qualifying rent increase.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 sets forth the financial interests from which a conflict of interest
may arise under the Act. With respect to the Rent Review Board's decision to make a non-binding
recommendation to resolve the dispute over the potential rent increase at Newport Apartments, the
facts presented provide that Chair Silva has a financial interest in Newport Apartments as a business
entity (Section 87103(a) and (d)) and as real property (Section 87103(b)). Additionally, the facts
presented indicate that Chair Silva has financial interests in Newport Apartments as a source of
income (Section 87103(c)), in the tenants of Newport Apartments as sources of income (ibid), and

File No. A-16-061
Page No. 3

in his personal finances and those of his immediate family (Regulation 18702.5(c)), and these
financial interests would also be implicated by the decision.

Foreseeability

Under the Act, a financial effect on a financial interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is
explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party
in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)

Chair Silva is financially interested in Newport Apartments as a business entity, as real property,
and as a source of income under Section 87103. And Newport Apartments is explicitly involved in the
Rent Review Board's decision to make a non-binding recommendation to resolve the dispute over
the potential rent increase at Newport Apartments. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a financial effect on Chair Silva's financial interests in Newport Apartments.

Materiality

Regulation 18702.1(a)(6) sets forth the applicable test for materiality and provides that the
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity in which an
official has a financial interest is material whenever the business entity is the subject of any
inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the regulatory authority of the official's agency.

Chair Silva is financially interested in Newport Apartments as a business entity, he is a
member of the Rental Review Board, and Newport Apartments is subject to a proceeding subject to
the regulatory authority of the Board. Therefore, the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Chair Silva's financial interest in Newport Apartments as a business
entity. Accordingly, Chair Silva is disqualified from participating in the decision under the Act.²

Appearance as a Member of the General Public

Regulation 18704 sets forth the definitions of "making a decision," "participating in a decision,"
and "using official position to attempt to influence a decision" for purposes of the Act. Regulation
18704(d)(2)(C) establishes an exception to those definitions, and provides as follows:

"(d) Exceptions. Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision does not include: . . .

"(2) Appearances as a Member of the General Public. An appearance by a public
official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing on matters related
solely to the his or her personal interests, including interests in: . . .

² It is unnecessary to address Chair Silva's financial interests in Newport Apartments as real property or as a
source of income, in the tenants of Newport Apartments as sources of income, or in his own personal finances and those
of his immediate family because there is a clear conflict of interest resulting from his interest in Newport Apartments as
a business entity.

File No. A-16-061
Page No. 4

"(C) A business entity over which the official, members of his or her immediate
family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family solely or
jointly exercise full direction and control."

Thus, a public official may appear as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of
its prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing on matters related solely to his or her
personal interests, including personal interests in a business entity over which the official and members
of his or her immediate family solely or jointly exercise full direction and control.

Chair Silva and his wife own a 60% stake in Newport Apartments. The Bylaws for Newport
Apartments provide that the majority owner controls its direction. Therefore, Chair Silva and his wife
jointly exercise full direction and control over Newport Apartments.

Accordingly, Chair Silva may appear as a member of the general public before the Rent
Review Board in its rent review hearing regarding a potential rent increase at Newport Apartments
pursuant to Regulation 18704(d)(2)(C). Otherwise, Chair Silva must comply with the procedures
for disqualification set forth in Section 87105 because he has a disqualifying conflict of interest
with respect to the decision under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

interest, given that he has a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding that decision under the Act?

Yes. Chair Silva may appear at the hearing as a member of the general public on matters 
related solely to his personal interests, but otherwise he must comply with the procedures for 
disqualification set forth in Section 87105.

in his personal finances and those of his immediate family (Regulation I 8702.S(c)), and these

Rent Review Board to represent himself in a rent review hearing regarding a potential rent increase 
at Newport Apartments, an apartment complex in which he and his wife have a 60% ownership 
interest, given that he has a disqualifying conflict of interest regarding that decision under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Chair Silva may appear at the hearing as a member of the general public on matters 
related solely to his personal interests, but otherwise he must comply with the procedures for 
disqualification set forth in Section 87105.
